author: @paulbooth7156
text: The pure carbon wick will burn to produce carbon dioxide, if it gets hot enough. The reason it does not burn is that the vaporizing oil cools the wick and prevents it from getting hot enough to burn. If you cut the wick too long and the oil cannot wick up fast enough, then the wick will burn down until it is small enough to balance the amount of oil it burns with the rate of wicking.

replies.author: @dongleseon8785
replies.text:
@paulbooth7156 which is exactly why normal cotton wicks doesn't burn off immediately. This only offset wick's self combustion threshold thus safety margin of irregular capillary speed.
The real solution is probably using fiber glass wicks which, at worst, melt and degrade.

author: @mcsmama
text:
Please keep in mind that carbon monoxide (CO) is given off as a byproduct of combustion when any fuel is burned.  If there is inadequate venting in a space & CO levels increase, you will breathe in CO, the CO will compete with OXYGEN for the hemoglobin in your blood, & the OXYGEN WILL LOSE!!!  
That is to say, if both CO & oxygen are present in your blood, your hemoglobin prefers to attach to CO over oxygen. 
Hemoglobin is the substance in your blood that transports oxygen to all of your tissues.  Too much CO available in a space leads to your hemoglobin becoming saturated with CO, which means there is zero ability left for oxygen to be delivered to your body tissues, which means you will die of carbon monoxide poisoning!!!  PLEASE UNDERSTAND THIS CONCEPT & be safe.  
If there is combustion, there MUST BE outside air & adequate venting, to get rid of CO.  Smaller spaces collect CO even faster than larger spaces.  People frequently die of accidental CO poisoning... you can't smell it, you can't taste it, you just go to sleep & never wake up.
God bless folks!😇
[11.26.2024]

author: @raindogs451
text: This is a faulty fad. You can't reimagine physics. Will hit generate heat? Yes. But less than a hair dryer. Much less.  There is no free lunch. In every one of these videos, Indoor Air Quality is not addressed as well.  Before this fad, some people used candles for ambience. And only later did they see the soot buildup. You cannot burn a fossil fuel; wood, charcoal, LP, NG, Oil candles, paraffin etc without products of combustion.  IAQ is impacted, and even those it's invisible, tasteless and odorless, you're breathing this. In the same way I'd keep batteries, flash lights and a wind up radio for emergencies, I might keep one of these setups.  You cannot not amplify BTUs; it defies the laws of physics. If you want one for emergencies, have at it. If you think, "I'll save money on my utility bill!", you'll  be cold and in an unhealthy indoor air environment until the house catches fire. You'll be warm then. IAQ doesn't improve.

author: @monicavonr9029
text:
Materialer basert på karbonfibrer har vist seg å være vanskelige å resirkulere, siden man ikke kan smelte dem om slik som med metaller, men i stedet må brenne eller kjemisk oppløse dem for å gjenvinne materialkomponentene.

Tidlige studier viste at slike fibrer i noen tilfeller kunne forårsake midlertidige betennelser i rottelunger ved høye konsentrasjoner, selv om langtidspåvirkningen på mennesker ikke synes å være tilstrekkelig kjent.

Karbonfibrer som baseres på nanorør, har imidlertid vist seg å kunne påvirke biologiske systemer i større grad. Slike nanorør er mye mindre, bare noen få nanometer i diameter, men kan bli flere millimeter lange. For eksempel har det blitt vist at flerveggede karbon-nanorør kan gi opphav til betennelser og asbest-lignende symptomer i mus, men man kjenner ikke fullstendig til hvordan disse fibrene påvirker mennesker.

source: https://snl.no/karbonfiber

author: @debbiethomas3687
text: Hi Robert.  I love your videos - only found you yesterday - and have just watched your video testing out different fuels (ethanol, Jay-Zee's mix etc., although I think they're a bit aggressive for my needs) but are you able to give us some info please on how cleanly the fuels (including the cooking oil and other heavier fuels) burn, please?  I use a plant pot heater with tealights to heat my greenhouse.  (For those who want a rough idea of how well they work, 3 tealights in a loaf tin raises the temperature in my well-insulated 6ft x 8ft greenhouse by 3-4C on a cold night.  The warmer the weather the less well they work - I don't know why.)  Trouble is, if I want to be in bed before midnight, I need to use 9-hour burn tealights.  If I have to light 2 or even all 3 heaters to maintain a minimum of 10C on a cold night a pack of 24 only lasts between 2 and 4 nights at best, so it gets quite expensive.  The good thing is they burn very cleanly and, as I'm growing cool/cold growing orchids, a clean burn is vital.  If I could use a carbon felt wick and some kind of clean burning fuel it could save me a lot of money, so any light you can throw on that would be brilliant.  Thanks.

text: Thank you for this video. Place pot upside down over “propane” or other safe fuels with tip end of  metal bolt directly and closely to heat source. Can regulate heat by raising and lowering flame, but keep flame in all lower settings. Be safe and warm… always have a smoke/carbon dioxide detector.

text: I worry a little bit about people who truly have little judgement about flammables, and will see a video like this and it's off to the races using all the wrong materials, trying different things, and then stuff happens. There are popup tents you can erect in your living room or bedroom, and you can cover it with blankets. Body heat will warm up the inside enough to be comfortable. Sleeping bags are essential in power outages too. YouTube has videos on popup in-home tents for power outages.

author: @KimGibsonfiberlover
text: I just found you, I love what I see!  This particular one is really interesting to me, since I have been burning vegetable (and fish) oil for years!  I do living history, and it turns out our rather smart forebears used a cotton wick in lots of oils as far back as (at least) the Bronze Age!  Just a simple wick in a shallow dish of oil.  Later they added water under the oil in a deeper dish to both prevent oil drips from the  edge the wick lay against, and to keep the bottom of the dish cool.  Coincidentally it self extinguishes.  So the idea of an everlasting wick is marvelous to me, and I think won’t be too obvious from 5 feet or so…. 😊

text: i have tried this and the problem i have is the combustion is incomplete. any air movement causes wisps of smoke, and anything you put it under will collect a lot of soot, which in turn will make heating less efficient. you can see a wisp of smoke come off whenever a draft disturbs the flame, and there's clearly emmisions when you put the metal tube on the plantpot at the end . so much ventilation is required in any kind of sheltered space that heat output becomes moot. as it is, this might well be a very dangerous stove for the unwise or unwary to operate, due to the carbon monoxide risk, but i think it might work better with a second stage like with a wood gasifying stove

author: @paulneilson6117
text:
It's also called witching because it was mysterious witchcraft that made the liquid rise up the paper.
I initially tried this with smaller diameter tubing 10 mm with alcohol. You will find that the wick will slowly burn even though its non flammable carbon felt. To prevent that you could silver plate the top 1 cm of carbon felt with silver that will NOT burn in air. Silver on carbon just might burn for years. If you're a bit fancier you could use platinum plating on carbon which I assure you will burn for quite some time.
Alternatively you could try a small stainless steel or silver mesh grill to keep the high heat away from the flame.

author: @aimeemoran7549
text: A chunk of cotton rope or cord supported in the oil also works fine and doesn’t itself burn up - it’s the basis of the “floating oil candles” that were very popular some time ago. All you really need is a ring around the “neck” of the cord (such as a grommet) which is used to both support the end of the cord above the oil, and also to contain the burning action at the end of the cord. We use these a lot while camping, as they’re self-extinguishing, nontoxic, and pretty.

author: @pipfox7834
text: If you can't find an UNGLAZED pot, one alternative that works is this: thick glass lampshades that people put out on the hardrubbish collections. I used one of these instead of a pot for many years, worked just fine. As soon as any cracking appears, change it for a newer one for safety reasons

author: @pipfox7834
text: one thing that is never mentioned: DO NOT use a glazed terracotta pot, otherwise you are just breathing in toxic fumes. Most pots ARE glazed, so do your due diligence and find a supplier of UNGLAZED pots. I have to repeat this on so many youtube sites (sigh) as the uploaders never bother to mention it.

replies.author: @pipfox7834
replies.text: glaze is transparent, so you have to really take a close look at the pot to make sure you are buying the right one. It should  have a slightly chalky, slightly rough surface - not smooth and shiny

author: @CondescendingOaf
text:
May need to make the hole in the planter bigger. There was still smoke coming out the top even with the extended metal chimney. 
Personally, I made an alcohol candle using a tea light candle holder (about the same size as a shot glass) and the cut off bottom of an aluminum soda can that happens to fit perfectly over the glass. Drilled about a 1/4 inch (4-5 mm) hole in the middle of the can bottom, then used a piece of pink fiber glass insulation that I rolled into a thin strip and fed through the hole to use as the wick. I use 90% isopropyl (rubbing) alcohol as the fuel. Depending on the size of the flame it will burn 2-4 hours on average. Also, if you blow the flame out before all of the alcohol burns, the fiberglass will last pretty much forever. However, if you let the alcohol burn out completely, it will "melt" (burn) the fiberglass. If that happens, just pull a bit more through and you're good to go. It takes such a small amount to make the wick. I got a couple chunks of the fiberglass  insulation out of a trash dumpster in a new home construction job site, that will probably last me a lifetime. 👍😁

replies.author: @CondescendingOaf
replies.text: @CelticDruidess1 Yes and no. You still need an exhaust hole large enough to let the extra air pass through. It will only take in as much air as it can let out. With the possible exception of it being some kind of pressurized system.

text: what im really interested in is if this thing would actually work on waste motor oil. I have a waste motoroil burner, but the problem with those kind of heaters is that they tend to be pretty high output. As in mine cant go below about 2L per hour, wich should according to a lot of source be about 20kw. Now lets say i harvest about 50% of that heat, still 10 kw IS A LOT of heat. My house central heating never even turns up to 10kw for heating the house. Now my shop is about 5 times as big, so it can do with the heat. But sometimes when its about 0 ℃ the shop even gets to 25℃+ and im running around in a tshirt and sweating big time.  So if i could make something like this with a wick i could probably have a pretty small heater to keep the temperature just level and not heat it up anymore.

author: @XD-te6vj
text:
Four tealight candles got the exterior of a terracotta pot up to 270°F (132°C), while a single candle got it up to 127°F (53°C).
It would take nearly 60 candles to effectively heat a 100-square-foot room.
You could boil water or even cook directly on the terracotta this way.
Heat is not “amplified” with these devices, only concentrated.
But that concentrated heat also comes with meaningful dangers.
That concentrated heat can be enough to warm small spaces.
So shrink your shelter space instead of expanding your candles.

replies.author: @stevenbingham3061
replies.text:
I would imagine that would be rather unlikely, because the convection flow of the air would cause cool air coming in from the bottom and the hot air being drawn out the top.  This should keep it sufficiently cool not to reach the flash point.  If the air at the bottom were restricted too much, the CO2 build up might extinguish the flame before a flash point could be reached.  
However, if I'm wrong make sure you have a good fire extinguisher handy, just in case.  🙂

@ThinkingandTinkering Yes but you *specifically remarked it won't burn :)*
Which it will. The very tip of the wick can overshoot 400°C with ease, especially if more air is provided with the aid of a chimney. 
The cotton wicks also transition from cellulose to carbon then to carbon dioxide as the combustion actually occurs im the carbon area only as there is the needed surface area for mixing the fuel vapor with air, react it and sustain the fuel decomposition temperature. 
Carbon felt wicks just skip the cellulose decomposition, they have larger fibres that will take longer to oxidize but eventually will.
  
By the way, in search of longer lasting wicks, glass fibres provide pretty good capillary action on higher viscosity fuels and silicon dioxide is... much harder to degrade. 
Woven gass cloth for composites, as an example. I used to run oil lamps in my early youth with glass wool pieces when I was irresponsibly exploring the catacombs in my city. They didn't burn very well but they were very hard to spill which was a good trait.

author: @hubrisnaut
text: That's cool. I could see how that could be made into standardized wick products. Wicking kerosene heaters are popular as backup heating option where I live. Vegetable oil is used in liquid "candles". There was a decorative product sold here in the US as a liquid candle "kit". I've made them as emergency candles when the power has gone out. Take a small glass jar and put veg oil in it (half full or so). Get a piece of thin plastic (like from a coffee can lid) and cut it into a disk so that it will comfortably fit in the jar leaving plenty of room to the sides of the jar (only want to cover half or less of the surface area of the oil). Punch a hole in the middle of the disk. Then you cut a short strip (one and a half inches or so.) of cotton (perhaps carbon felt) from an old t-shirt or something, roll lengthwise and run it through the hole leaving half an inch or so on one side, which will be the top side. Then you put the disc and wick on the surface of the oil so it floats short wick side up. Key is to gently push it down so the oil covers the plastic disk, and it will. There you go! A liquid "candle... Light the wick once it becomes saturated. As the oil burns down the wick stays floating on top.

author: @johndough8115
text:
Adding a Chimney, does not change the "Soot" problem.  The soot, will just collect on the Chimney walls... and eventually, may in fact cause a Chimney fire...

 The only way to eliminate soot, is to make a Rocket-Stove design, that has the correct amount of airflow, to allow a 100% clean burn.   The burn will be MUCH hotter than a typical fire... and so, you will need much more robust solutions, than a Tin-Chimney.   IE:  Thick metal pipes.  Or...  maybe something like High Heat capable fire-brick.

 From what Ive read... certain rocket stoves and or rocket mass heaters... have eventually worn holes in the steel combustion sections.  The thicker the steel, the longer it will survive.

 Poorly designed rocket stoves / rocket mass heaters, do produce smoke / soot.  Again, it is about the shape, lengths, and the amount of airflow needed... to make sure that the fire gets hot enough, to burn completely.

 As for veggie oil not burning... Im not so sure about that.  I was in a house, when these dudes nearly burned the place down.  They were trying to fry stuff, got the oil too hot... and it burst into some seriously tall flames. That said... Im not sure what cooking oil, that they were using.

 It might not be easy to light on fire... but once its going... it can be quite a problem.

 I was using an Outdoor  Propane powered Wok Burner.  I accidentally let the oil get too hot,  and the oil ignited,
and blasted into an explosive fireball that was about 7ft high in the air.  Luckily, I was always prepared... such as pre-wetting the ground around the Wok.  And luckily, it wasnt that hard to turn off the burner, and cover it with the Lid.  I believe I was using Peanut oil.

 Veggie oil, is almost always "Soybean" oil  (which isnt really good for you).  Even worse for you, when its Hydrogenated as that stuff sticks to your internals, like Glue.

author: @TheRebelmanone
text:
The chimney looked like it worked great with the wick by itself, but after you put the flowerpot over the wick and then put the chimney on top of that, it began smoking again. Maybe the pot itself is enough to act as the chimney, i am not sure why it began smoking after the long pipe was added on top. I would have thought it would create a better draft and cause it to burn even cleaner, but maybe it is too much draft and blowing air past the flame so fast it has no time to heat up in the chimney enough to burn that smoke off.

I guess i wonder about it because i am wondering if there is a way to make it like a rocket stove. Have the flame inside an insulated chamber designed to get hot and stay hot, and then how they have that 55 gallon drum forcing the exhaust back down around the outside of the burn chamber to then the exhaust goes into some pipes that go thru some mass, and then finally the pipe exits the building. But in this case with the tiny little wicks, to just have a few of them burning in a tiny rocket stove burn chamber, and see what the exhaust looks like, if it is clean or not. Maybe it is clean enough to allow to exit inside the home? But also in this case the rocket stove will be miniature, the 55 gallon drum could be like a one gallon paint can, etc... And with those wicks having the possibility to last forever, if the oil burn chamber part is designed right, all you would need to do is add veg oil and mess with nothing else, just light it again. I say "like" a one gallon paint can, meaning i would suggest using real 1/4" thick SS where the exhaust heat hits the bottom of the can/drum otherwise it will burn out prematurely, and also with that thick plate getting the brunt of the heat  it can be used as a cook top.  But for mock up you could try a paint can just to see how everything behaves(the flame, the soot, the exhaust heat, etc...), but in a test environment, not in a home yet, and see how it behaves over time first. Maybe i could try, i can make most of it out of cob for a test, and then paint cans, some fire insulation, and pipes. But the pipes and fire insulation will be used later, those are the only things bought that i need in the mockup that i will also use later in the actual installation, once/if i go to be serious about installing it somewhere, after i confirmed it will work. If it don't work good enough i will just use wood as the fuel and exhaust it outside after it goes past the cooktop and thru the mass, just like traditionally. So all the materials i used on the mockup can be taken off and used to install it permanent, besides the stuff i don't want in a permanent installation like the paint cans, and the extra cobb. I could still have some cobb though, it can be beneficial, run the pipes thru it, and it is cheap(free really). The permanent stove will be SS burn chamber with fire brick and insulation, with that thick cooktop.

author: @justinw1765
text:
Right off the bat, I would make at least one quick tweak. I would insulate the copper, so that the oil gets hotter and burns more efficiently and fully. You want the copper to conduct the heat all through the oil, but at the same time, you don't want it conducting the heat away from the oil out into the surroundings. As you have noted in other videos, not only is vegetable oil dense and viscous, but they tend to have high ignition/combustion temps. The shape and surface area could probably be played around with to increase efficiency a bit. 

  (Tip, unrefined oils tend to have significantly lower ignition/combustion temps).

text:
Haha, I actually mentioned/recommended carbon felt to a person commenting on one of your prior videos--like yesterday or the day before.

If you combine the principles of using those vortex type stoves (two cans of a different size within each other, with offset holes on bottom and/or top), insulating the stove and using copper to conduct heat, using multiple carbon felt wicks, and using a chimney, you can create a burner that burns various different waxes quite efficiently and cleanly.

replies.author: @Chimel31
replies.text:
You can clearly see the soot over the chimney, so you probably need to improve the draft to create a complete combustion, together with a double wall combustion chamber to raise the heat even further. Basically, a rocket stove, but using oil instead of wood. Much better than a rocket stove, actually, because instead of constantly feeding wood, it's easy to get a constant flow of oil from a big drum of recycled cooking oil.
Another issue with such open fires is the risk of emitting deadly carbon monoxide, or even plain carbon dioxide. The most advanced gas heaters get an external air supply and exhaust, it's the hot ceramic material that radiates the infrared heat, and the room air that enters the base of the heater and come out warm on top, without ever getting in direct contact with the flame. I'll try it out. This may not replace my wood stove, but I reckon it could be great to heat the glass house in winter and spring for my citrus and tomato seedlings...

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text:
The diameter of the microscopic passageways and pores in the material does affect the height you can draw something to - the smaller the better - but it also reduces the flow in each micro-tube. If you need to deliver more fuel in total, you would need to increase the cross sectional area of material used. But those two factors are independent - changing the overall diameter won't affect what happens at the microscopic level, just how much of it. Now if you compress the material, that might be a different story.
But as they say - all the theory in the world can be undone by a single experiment. ;)

replies.author: @newmonengineering
replies.text: The tube works by itself better because it is closer to the flame so it draws air faster around the sides, more hot air for longer = clean burn. The pot is so large that only ambient air flow through the hole is happening so it has less flow. The key is more hot air for a longer time to eliminate the soot. If you put a blower or fan under the pot it would still be clear. Hope that helps.

replies.author: @tichrontus
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Not sure if this helps much:
My grandparents were buddhists and they had an altar with oil lamps. I used to fiddle around with the wick in it and noticed there's a sweet spot. Too short, the flame goes out. Too long and the wick burns out faster. Didn't think too much about it til i was much older. Apparently there are a couple of factors at play here. Apart from the ones u mentioned, there's flash point, flame speed and autoignition temperature. Just a hypothesis, if u stick the wick in a thermally conductive sleeve (maybe steel), it helps with vapourization. Having the tip of the wick just below the sleeve's rim will minimize the wick's contact with oxygen. In short, u want the fuel to vapourize as fast as possible yet only ignite away from it.
